analogy: Think of "South Asia" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview In reading about world politics, we frequently encounter the terms ‘security’ or ‘national security’. Often, they are used to stop debate and discussion. We hear that an issue is a security issue and that it is vital for the well-being of the country.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1994 UNDP’s Human Development Report, which contends,, STEPS, South Asia, South. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
South Asia is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that o verview In reading about world politics, we frequently encounter the terms ‘security’ or ‘national security’.

The text explains that often, they are used to stop debate and discussion.

The text explains that we hear that an issue is a security issue and that it is vital for the well-being of the country.

The text explains that the implication is that it is too important or secret to be debated and discussed openly.

The text explains that we see movies in which everything surrounding ‘national security’ is shadowy and dangerous.

The text explains that security seems to be something that is not the business of the ordinary citizen.

The text explains that in a democracy, surely this cannot be the case.

The text explains that as citizens of a democracy, we need to know more about the term security.

The text explains that it introduces two different ways of looking at security and highlights the importance of keeping in mind different contexts or situations which determine our view of security.

The text explains that chapter 5 Security in the Contemporary World The concern about human security was reflected in the 1994 UNDP’s Human Development Report, which contends, “the concept of security has for too long been interpreted narrowly… It has been more related to nation states than people… Forgotten were the legitimate concerns of ordinary people who sought security in their daily lives.” The images above show various forms of security threats.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 64 What is security?

The text explains that at its most basic, security implies freedom from threats.

The text explains that human existence and the life of a country are full of threats.

The text explains that does that mean that every single threat counts as a security threat?

The text explains that every time a person steps out of his or her house, there is some degree of threat to their existence and way of life.

The text explains that our world would be saturated with security issues if we took such a broad view of what is threatening.

The text explains that those who study security, therefore, generally say that only those things that threaten ‘core values’ should be regarded as being of interest in discussions of security.

The text explains that the core values of ordinary women and men in the street?

The text explains that do governments, on behalf of citizens, always have the same notion of core values as the ordinary citizen?

The text explains that furthermore, when we speak of threats to core values, how intense should the threats be?

The text explains that every time another country does something or fails to do something, this may damage the core values of one’s country.

The text explains that s o w e a r e b r o u g h t t o a conclusion: security relates only to extremely dangerous threats— threats that could so endanger core values that those values would be damaged beyond repair if we did not do something to deal with the situation.

The text explains that and, at any given time in world history, do all societies have the same conception of security?

The text explains that the source of this danger is another country which by threatening military action endangers the core values of sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity.

The text explains that in responding to the threat of war, a government has three basic choices: to surrender; to prevent the other side from attacking by promising to raise the costs of war to an unacceptable level; and to defend itself when war actually breaks out so as to deny the attacking country its objectives and to turn back or defeat the attacking forces altogether.

The text explains that governments may choose to surrender when actually confronted by war, but they will not advertise this as the policy of the country.

The text explains that governments are, therefore, very sensitive to the balance of power between their country and other countries.

The text explains that that is because the international system is a rather brutal arena in which there is no central authority capable of controlling behaviour.

The text explains that within a country, the threat of violence is regulated by an acknowledged central authority — the government.

The text explains that how can a society be secure if there is violence or the threat of violence inside its borders?

The text explains that the reason it is not given so much importance is that after the Second World War it seemed that, for the most powerful countries on earth, internal security was more or less assured.

The text explains that while internal security was certainly a p a r t o f t h e c o n c e r n s o f governments historically, after the Second World War there was a context and situation in which internal security did not seem to matter as much as it had in the past.

The text explains that it is now an almost universally-accepted view that countries should only go to war for the right reasons, primarily self-defence or to protect other people from genocide.

The text explains that while it did allow both countries to deploy a very limited number of defensive systems, it stopped them from large-scale production of those systems.

The text explains that the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) of 1968 was an arms control treaty in the sense that it regulated the acquisition o f n u c l e a r w e a p o n s : t h o s e countries that had tested and manufactured nuclear weapons before 1967 were allowed to keep their weapons; and those that had not done so were to give up the right to acquire them.

The text explains that confidence building is a process in which countries share ideas and information with their rivals.

The text explains that in short, confidence building is a process designed to ensure that rivals do not go to war through misunderstanding or misperception.

The text explains that they begin by questioning the traditional referent of security.

The text explains that in doing so, they also question the other three elements of security — what is being secured, from what kind of threats and the approach to security.

The text explains that indeed, during the last 100 years, more people have been killed by their own governments than by foreign armies.

The text explains that since these problems are global in nature, international cooperation is vital, even though it is difficult to achieve. neW sources of threats The non-traditional conceptions— both human security and global security—focus on the changing nature of threats to security.

The text explains that c i v i l i a n t a r g e t s a r e usually chosen to terrorise the public and to use the unhappiness of the public as a weapon against national governments or other parties in conflict.

The text explains that since 11 September 2001 when terrorists attacked the World Trade Centre in America, other governments and public have paid more attention to terrorism, though terrorism itself is not new.

The text explains that human rights have come to be classified into three types.

The text explains that the first type is political rights such as freedom of speech and assembly.

The text explains that the second type is economic and social rights.

The text explains that the third type is the rights of colonised people or ethnic and indigenous minorities.

The text explains that while there is broad agreement on this classification, there is no agreement on which set of rights should be considered as universal Why do we always look outside when talking about human rights violations?

The text explains that security in the Contemporary World 73 human rights, nor what the international community should do when rights are being violated.

The text explains that since the 1990s, developments such as Iraq’s invasion of Kuwait, the genocide in Rwanda, and the Indonesian military’s killing of people in East Timor have led to a debate on whether or not the UN should intervene to stop human rights abuses.

The text explains that there are those who argue that the UN Charter empowers the inter national community to take up arms in defence of human rights.

The text explains that is terrorism a traditional or non-traditional threat to security?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 5: South Asia
